subject: logic
question: I'm going to read you four statements, and exactly one of them is true - your job is to tell me which. Statement A says: exactly one of these four statements is false. Statement B says: exactly two of them are false. Statement C says: exactly three of them are false. And statement D says: all four of them are false. So - which one is the true one?
modelReasoningPath: 1. Translate the premise: if exactly one statement is true, then exactly three are false. 2. Which statement says "exactly three are false"? That's C - so C would be the true one, and that's self-consistent. 3. Sanity-check the others as the lone truth: A says one is false (would mean three true - contradicts "only one true"); B says two false (would mean two true - contradiction); D says four false (would mean none true, including itself - contradiction). Only C survives.  The skill is connecting the premise ("exactly one true") to its arithmetic consequence ("exactly three false") and finding the statement that asserts exactly that.

rubric.strong: Reasons "one true means three false, and C says three false, so C" - fast, from the self-referential structure. Can show why A/B/D each contradict themselves as the sole truth.
rubric.developing: Gets there by testing statements one at a time (often needs to be walked through A collapsing first), then applies the method to the rest. Sound once the testing method is modelled.
rubric.weak: Picks a statement with no test of consistency, or can't see that "exactly one true" fixes the number of false ones. Doesn't engage the self-reference.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not converting "exactly one true" into "exactly three false."
commonMistakes.reveals: what this reveals about their thinking

commonMistakes.mistake: Checking each statement's wording in isolation without testing it as the unique true one.
commonMistakes.reveals: what this reveals about their thinking

commonMistakes.mistake: Picking D because "most false" feels dramatic.
commonMistakes.reveals: what this reveals about their thinking

liveProbes.probe: If exactly one statement is true, how many are false?
Suppose A were the true one - would that hold together?

hints: "If exactly one statement is true, then how many of the four are false?"
"Now find the statement that claims that exact number of false ones."
"Test it - does that statement being the only true one stay consistent? Try A as the true one and watch it fall apart, then do the same with the rest."
